title: Customer-support agent resolution dropped from 70% to 55% over two weeks. Diagnose it.
clarifying_questions: Is the drop confirmed in the data, or based on one customer's complaint? Anecdote vs signal.
Sudden cliff or gradual decline? Cliff suggests a release/event; gradual suggests drift.
Is it one customer, a segment, or all customers?
Did we ship anything in two weeks — prompt change, model swap, KB update, integration change?
Did the model provider push a silent update? Hosted LLMs change quietly.
Is the definition of 'resolved' stable, or did escalation logic change?
user_segments: Enterprise customers running the support agent
End-users raising support tickets across channels
CS/CSM teams owning escalation logic
Data team owning the resolution-rate pipeline

framework.name: Decompose-scope-hypothesize-validate RCA
steps: Decompose the metric into its chain of steps before hypothesizing
Scope the drop by customer, intent, channel, time/geo
Generate hypotheses in three buckets: internal/product, external/customer, data/tracking
Prioritize by likelihood x ease-to-check
Validate by re-running evals and tracing failed cases
Recommend immediate fix + a durable regression guardrail

key_pointers: Resolution rate isn't atomic — find the moved term before hypothesizing causes
A 15-point end-to-end drop can come from a 3-point drop in one step compounding
Silent model-provider updates are the most common 2025/26 cause — check the changelog and re-run evals
Distinguish a real quality drop from a tracking-definition change
Trace ~50 failed cases manually to find the modal failure mode
RCA on AI products must extend beyond your code to the model, data, and upstream environment

answer.heading: Decompose the metric first
answer.content: Resolution rate isn't atomic. For an agentic support workflow it's a chain: Resolution rate = Intent-classification accuracy x Retrieval accuracy x Generation quality x Action-execution success x User acceptance (didn't escalate). A 15-point end-to-end drop can come from a single 3-point regression in any one step compounding through the chain. So I don't hypothesize yet — I find the moved term first.

answer.heading: Scope the drop
content: By customer/segment — one enterprise, one industry, or all of them?
By ticket type/intent — are some categories degrading more (e.g. a customer's new release creating unseen questions)?
By channel — email vs chat vs voice; if only one, the issue is likely upstream of the core agent.
By time-of-day/geography — anything pointing to data, latency, or provider behavior.

answer.heading: Three buckets of hypotheses
content: Internal/product: silent model regression, a prompt change in the last release, stale vector index, a cost-saving model swap downgrading quality, a broken tool/integration failing the action step, or tightened confidence thresholds causing more escalations (a false 'drop' in resolved).

External/customer-side: customer launched a new product creating uncovered ticket types, changed their own KB/policies without notice, a seasonal spike in unusual queries, a new messier channel, or a changed CSAT/escalation definition.
Data/tracking: the 'resolution' definition changed in the pipeline, a logging change is missing auto-resolved cases, sampling bias, or a bug double-counting escalations.

rows: Check provider changelog + run eval suite vs baseline
Diff last 2 weeks of releases, re-run evals on changed prompts
Retrieval accuracy by customer; ask CS for flagged changes
Cluster new tickets by topic vs KB coverage

answer.heading: Validate
content: Re-run the production eval suite — same degradation, or production-specific?
Pull 50 failed cases from the last week and trace each through the workflow to find the modal failure mode.
If a provider update is suspected, A/B against the locked previous model version on the failed traces.

Define success: resolution returns to >=68% within 7 days, evals show no regression on the failing set, no new escalation spike, and false-resolution rate (resolved cases returning as new tickets within 7 days) doesn't rise.

answer.heading: Recommendation and senior insight
answer.content: Two patterns are most likely. Pattern A — silent model regression (most common): lock the underlying model to the previous version for affected workflows, re-run evals, give the customer a forensic root cause within 48 hours, and build a regression eval suite that runs on every provider update. Pattern B — KB/customer drift: trigger a KB refresh and re-index, then add a continuous KB-freshness check plus alerting on customer-side content changes. The senior point: in agentic systems, 'nothing changed in our product' usually means nothing changed in our code — but the model under the hood, the data on both sides, and the customer's reality change constantly. RCA on AI products must extend beyond the codebase.

strong_vs_generic.strong: Decomposes resolution into a multiplicative chain, finds the moved term, and prioritizes the silent model update before jumping to fixes.
strong_vs_generic.generic: Immediately blames 'the model got worse' or 'users are angrier' without decomposing or checking tracking.

title: Recruiting candidate quality dropped after a model update. Diagnose the failure mode.
clarifying_questions: Was this a model swap (different provider) or a version update (e.g. Claude 3.7 → Claude 4)? Different debugging paths.
How is 'candidate quality' defined — ranking accuracy, recruiter accept rate, interview rate, time-to-hire?
Did our eval suite catch this before deployment? If not, that's a separate problem.
Is the drop uniform across roles or concentrated (tech vs ops, senior vs junior, US vs India)?
Did anything else change besides the model — prompts, embeddings, scoring weights?
Are explanations also degrading, or only the ranking?
user_segments: Recruiters reviewing top-K candidate shortlists
Hiring managers running interviews
Enterprise customers with role-specific needs
Compliance owners tracking adverse-impact

framework.name: Stage-decomposed model-swap RCA
steps: Confirm what changed (swap vs version) — the search space is already smaller
Decompose the matching workflow into stages the model touches
Generate hypotheses: model-behavior, prompt-model mismatch, system integration
Validate by running the new model on historical evals and tracing failed candidates old-vs-new
Recommend rollback + regression suite + per-workflow model pinning
Add counter-metrics including adverse-impact and explanation hallucination

key_pointers: This is a smaller search space than an open RCA — we know the model changed, so go deep on the failure mode
Decompose the workflow: JD parsing, candidate parsing, semantic matching, ranking/scoring, explanation
A new model can parse fields differently, grade more strictly, shift bias, or break few-shot generalization
If the eval suite didn't catch it, the eval set was incomplete — fix that too
Pin each workflow to the model that scores highest on its own eval suite (routing layer)
Treat every model update as a release with full eval gating, never a transparent upgrade

answer.heading: Why this RCA differs
answer.content: Unlike an open 'metric dropped, find the cause' problem, here we already know what changed — the model. The search space is smaller, so the work is deeper diagnosis: which stage of the matching workflow did the new model degrade, and why? First I'd confirm whether it was a provider swap or a version bump, since they imply different debugging paths.

answer.heading: Decompose where a model swap can hurt

headers: How a new model can break it
rows: Different fit judgments; embeddings may also have swapped
Harsher/looser grading shifts the score distribution
Tell recruiter why it's a match

answer.heading: Three buckets of hypotheses
content: Model-behavior: new model is stricter so fewer 'high-match' candidates surface (weaker top-K); tokenization differences hurt certain resume formats; few-shot examples don't generalize; different bias along seniority/industry/geography.
Prompt-model mismatch: prompts were tuned for the old model's style; structured-output prompts (JSON, rubrics) behave differently; chain-of-thought length/structure no longer fits.
System integration: embeddings model also swapped, breaking upstream retrieval; scoring threshold calibration didn't carry over; downstream weights expected the old model's score distribution.

answer.heading: Validate
content: Run the new model on the historical eval set — does it match or fail? If it fails, the eval set was incomplete (a separate fix).
Sample 50 candidates where recruiters say quality dropped; trace each through the workflow with old and new model side by side to find the divergence.
A/B test on live traffic with a 20% holdout on the old model to isolate the model effect from environmental noise.

answer.heading: Recommendation and senior insight
answer.content: Immediate (24h): roll the affected workflow back to the previous model, give affected customers a clear forensic explanation, and pause auto-rollout of model updates pending stronger evals. Short-term (1-2 wks): build a recruiting-specific model-swap regression suite, then either re-tune prompts for the new model and re-test, or decide the old model is fine for this workflow and pin it. Strategic (1-3 mo): use a routing layer so every workflow runs on the model that scores highest on its own eval suite — don't force one model on all workflows. The senior point: model updates aren't free; the benefit is sometimes capability or cost, but the cost is always regression risk. Treat every update as a release with full eval gating, never a transparent upgrade.

strong_vs_generic.strong: Decomposes the matching workflow stage-by-stage, isolates the failure with old-vs-new traces, and lands on per-workflow model pinning with eval gating.
strong_vs_generic.generic: Says 'the new model is worse, roll back' without locating the stage or proposing a durable gating policy.

title: Average order value on your e-commerce platform drops 18% week-over-week. How do you diagnose and address it?
clarifying_questions: Is the drop global or segment-specific (category, geo, device, new vs returning)?
Could this be a data issue — instrumentation error, tagging change, attribution shift?
Did AOV's denominator move — are orders up while revenue is flat, or revenue down with flat orders?
Did anything ship this week — pricing experiment, coupon campaign, feed-ranking update?
What's our weekly order volume, to quantify revenue at risk?
Is the 18% drop sudden (one week) or the latest point in a trend?
user_segments: New vs returning buyers
High-AOV category shoppers (electronics) vs low-AOV (grocery)
Promo-driven / discount-sensitive buyers
Device cohorts (app vs web, premium vs budget)

framework.name: Scope-rule out data-decompose-hypothesize-act RCA
steps: Scope the drop — global or segment-specific (category, geo, device, cohort)?
Rule out data issues — instrumentation, tagging, attribution
Break down AOV = revenue / orders — which side moved?
Hypothesize causes — mix shift, promo spike, supply issue, algo change
Run a 48-hour diagnostic checklist
Propose a solution matched to the root cause

key_pointers: Don't jump to solutions — exhaust diagnosis first; interviewers reward methodical thinkers
Sharpest first move: segment AOV by category (electronics vs fashion vs grocery have wildly different norms)
Always ask 'did anything change this week?' — pricing test, coupon campaign, or feed ranking often explains it
Quantify business impact early: 18% AOV drop x 1M orders/week is significant revenue at risk
Check the counter-metric: if AOV drops but orders surge, net revenue can still be up
Decompose AOV into its parts before hypothesizing

answer.heading: Establish urgency and scope
answer.content: First quantify the stakes: an 18% AOV drop on ~1M orders/week is material revenue at risk, so this is urgent. Then scope: is the drop global or concentrated in a category, geography, device, or new-vs-returning cohort? Before any of that, rule out a data artifact — a tagging change, attribution shift, or instrumentation bug can fake an 18% move.

answer.heading: Decompose AOV
content: AOV = revenue / orders. Determine which side moved.
If orders rose while revenue stayed flat → likely a mix shift to cheaper items or a discount surge.
If revenue fell with flat orders → likely a supply issue in high-value categories or a pricing change.
Segment AOV by category first — electronics vs fashion vs grocery have very different AOV norms, so a category mix shift can move the blended number without any single category changing.

rows: Orders flat/up, units same, category mix shifted
AOV by category + category share of orders
Shipped this week, exposure shifted to cheap items
Drop only in one tracking source

answer.heading: 48-hour diagnostic checklist
content: Confirm the drop in a second data source to rule out instrumentation.
Pull AOV trend by category, channel, device, and new-vs-returning cohort.
Diff the week's releases: pricing experiments, coupon campaigns, feed-ranking/recommendation changes.
Check discount depth and promo redemption rates WoW.
Check availability of top-AOV SKUs/categories.
Check order-volume counter-metric: did orders surge enough to keep net revenue flat or up?

rows: AOV segmented by category, channel, and cohort
If orders surge, net revenue may still be up

answer.heading: Recommendation
answer.content: Diagnose before you treat. If the root cause is a mix shift to lower-priced items, lift AOV back with 'frequently bought together' bundles and a minimum-order-for-free-delivery threshold. If it's a promo spike, tighten discount eligibility. If it's a supply gap in high-value categories, prioritize restocking. And always read AOV alongside order volume — an 18% AOV drop paired with a large order surge may be a healthy acquisition outcome, not a problem.

strong_vs_generic.strong: Quantifies revenue at risk, rules out data, decomposes AOV, and segments by category first before proposing a cause-matched fix.
strong_vs_generic.generic: Immediately suggests 'run more bundles and upsells' without diagnosing whether revenue or orders moved.

title: How would you measure the success of a new 'Buy Again' shortcut on Zepto's home screen?
clarifying_questions: What exactly does 'Buy Again' do — surface past orders on the home screen, tap to re-add to cart?
What's the goal — engagement, incremental orders, or retention lift?
Which users see it — all users or only those with prior orders?
Is the bar incremental orders, or just moving existing intent to a new surface?
Are there concerns about cannibalizing search/discovery?
Can we run an A/B test with a holdout to measure incremental lift?
user_segments: Returning users with 2+ prior orders (core target)
Frequent reorderers (staples, groceries)
New/low-history users (little to surface)
Browsers who rely on discovery/search

framework.name: Funnel + incrementality measurement
steps: Clarify the feature — 'Buy Again' surfaces past orders; tap re-adds to cart
Define success — engagement, incremental orders, or retention lift
Map the funnel — impression → click → add-to-cart → checkout → order placed
Layer the retention angle — does it raise order frequency for returning users?
Set guardrails — cannibalization of discovery, smaller baskets
Design an A/B test with holdout to measure incremental lift

key_pointers: Don't just list metrics — show funnel logic from impression to order placed
The feature only succeeds if it creates incremental orders, not just moves existing intent to a new surface
Distinguish engagement metrics (CTR) from impact metrics (incremental order rate, frequency lift)
Call out cannibalization risk: less catalog exploration can hurt LTV even while boosting short-term conversion
Watch basket size — reorder-one-item behavior can shrink baskets vs browsing for five
Use a holdout to isolate true incremental lift

answer.heading: Define what 'success' means
answer.content: 'Buy Again' surfaces a returning user's past orders on the home screen; one tap re-adds items to the cart. The feature is only a success if it generates incremental orders — not if it merely relocates intent a user already had (they'd have searched for the same items anyway). So I'd separate engagement metrics from genuine impact, and lean on a holdout to prove incrementality.

rows: % of eligible users shown the shortcut

answer.heading: Primary metric and experiment
answer.content: Primary: Incremental orders per week attributed to 'Buy Again' clicks, measured via an A/B test (treatment with the shortcut vs a holdout control). Success is a statistically significant lift in orders for the treatment group — proof the feature creates demand rather than just rerouting it. I'd also track the 7-day repeat-purchase rate for the cohort that uses it vs non-users to confirm a frequency lift.

rows: Less discovery can hurt long-term LTV
Order frequency lift for returning users
The real value of a reorder shortcut

answer.heading: The cannibalization trap
content: If 'Buy Again' reduces catalog exploration, users discover fewer new products → narrower baskets and weaker long-term LTV, even as short-term conversion looks great.
A user who reorders 1 staple instead of browsing and buying 5 items is a revenue downgrade hiding behind a higher CTR.
Net it out: judge the feature on incremental orders and basket size together, not CTR alone.

answer.heading: Activation
answer.content: Activation event: a returning user with 2+ prior orders taps 'Buy Again' and completes checkout. Track the 7-day repeat-purchase rate for this activated cohort vs feature non-users — if activated users buy again sooner and more often, the shortcut is building the habit it was designed for.

strong_vs_generic.strong: Centers on incremental orders via a holdout, separates CTR from impact, and watches basket-size and discovery cannibalization.
strong_vs_generic.generic: Calls a high click-through rate on the shortcut 'success' without testing incrementality or LTV impact.

title: How would you measure the success of Meesho's newly launched in-app video shopping feature?
clarifying_questions: Is the goal discovery, conversion, engagement, or all three?
Where does video appear — home feed, search results, or product pages?
Which categories are in scope first — fashion, home, electronics?
Are we optimizing for new-user trust or returning-user conversion?
What counts as a video-assisted session for attribution?
Can we run a holdout to compare 30-day GMV and retention?
user_segments: New vs returning users
Tier 1 vs Tier 2/3 (price-sensitive) buyers
Category cohorts (fashion vs home)
High-intent searchers vs casual browsers

framework.name: Stage-layered funnel with holdout
steps: Clarify the goal — discovery, conversion, engagement, or all three
Define the funnel — impressions → play → watch time → product tap → add to cart → purchase
Layer metrics by stage — awareness, engagement, conversion, retention
Segment the analysis — new vs returning, Tier 1 vs Tier 2/3, category
Set guardrails — what tells you the feature is hurting the core experience
Propose a holdout experiment comparing 30-day GMV and retention

key_pointers: Don't just list metrics — rank them: primary = GMV influenced by video, secondary = engagement depth
Meesho's base is price-sensitive Tier 2/3 — video may build trust before it lifts conversion, so acknowledge the lag
Best answers propose a holdout: control sees no video, test does — compare 30-day GMV and retention
Define a clean attribution rule for 'video-assisted' sessions (>10s watched before purchase)
Guardrail against video increasing scroll depth while killing conversion
Surface video to high-intent users first for fast, clean signal

answer.heading: Clarify the goal and rank intent
answer.content: Video shopping can serve discovery, engagement, or conversion — I'd confirm the primary goal, then rank metrics accordingly: GMV influenced by video as primary, engagement depth as secondary. The key nuance is Meesho's audience: price-sensitive Tier 2/3 buyers may use video to build trust in a product before they convert, so early wins may show up as engagement and retention before they show up as conversion. I'd design measurement to capture that lag rather than declare failure prematurely.

answer.heading: Primary metric and experiment
answer.content: Primary: GMV attributed to video-assisted sessions — sessions where the user watched >10s of video before purchase. Validate it with a holdout: a control group sees no video, a test group does, and we compare 30-day GMV and retention. This isolates the feature's incremental contribution and, critically, gives the trust-building lag time to show up in 30-day numbers rather than session-level conversion alone.

answer.heading: Segmentation
content: New vs returning — video may matter more for new users who need trust before buying.
Tier 1 vs Tier 2/3 — expect a trust-then-convert lag in price-sensitive Tier 2/3 cohorts.
Category — fashion (visual, video-friendly) vs home; effect size will differ.
High-intent vs casual — surfacing video in search results targets high-intent users for cleaner early signal.

rows: If video raises scroll depth but kills conversion, it's net negative

answer.heading: Activation / rollout for clean signal
answer.content: Surface video thumbnails in search results first (not the home feed). High-intent users searching for a specific item are more likely to engage and convert, which produces a clean, fast signal on whether video lifts conversion — before rolling it into the broader, noisier home feed.

strong_vs_generic.strong: Ranks GMV-from-video as primary, runs a 30-day holdout, and explicitly models the Tier 2/3 trust-then-convert lag.
strong_vs_generic.generic: Lists watch time and play rate as success without attributing GMV or accounting for the conversion lag.
